concerns.text: The plan proposes using synthetic data to validate a pipeline intended to predict 'time-to-peak softening' based on specific physical interactions (cold work, solute effects). Synthetic data generated from 'established physical models' (e.g., Avrami) will inherently possess the exact mathematical relationships the model is supposed to discover. This creates a tautological validation loop where the model is tested on data it was designed to fit, failing to assess generalization to real-world experimental noise, measurement error, or unmodeled physical phenomena. The methodology cannot validate the claim that the model captures 'non-linear, interactive effects' in real materials if the ground truth is artificially constructed to match those effects.

The sensitivity analysis plan ('Sweep prediction confidence interval (±5%, ±10%, ±15%)') is methodologically misaligned with the research question. The spec aims to predict a specific kinetic outcome (time-to-peak). Sweeping the *prediction confidence interval* (a post-hoc statistical metric) does not test the model's robustness to input variability (e.g., how does a ±5% error in cold work measurement affect the predicted time?). This approach measures the stability of the error metric itself rather than the sensitivity of the physical prediction to input uncertainty, failing to address the 'robustness' requirement in US-3.

The plan states: 'Interaction terms... will be considered *only if* VIF < 5.' This is a methodological error. Variance Inflation Factor (VIF) measures multicollinearity among *predictors*. Interaction terms (e.g., ColdWork * Mg) are often highly correlated with their constituent main effects by definition. A strict VIF < 5 threshold on interaction terms will systematically exclude the very non-linear interactions the hypothesis claims are critical, leading to a model that is structurally incapable of answering the research question.
